cards.tip: WIN — Robert used the car analogy—'imagine driving cross country then immediately back'—to explain the compressor strain when the customer resisted spending money on a new unit, which converted the cost concern into a maintenance commitment.

FIX — When the customer said a competing installer had done three units cheaper, you said 'we do things the right way,' but didn't ask what mattered more: price or peace of mind? Reconnect to their actual priority instead of defending.

WIN — When the customer hesitated about replacing now, Andrew backed his warning with specific amps data (56.5 to 74.5, up 150% YoY) and said "If it's gone up from 50 to 75 in a year, I don't know how much time you guys got on that compressor, man"; he sealed the flip by immediately offering to book tomorrow's estimate, emphasizing calendar urgency ("we'll probably get booked up tomorrow")—turning hesitation into commitment.

FIX — When she said she had a 10am deadline, that was the moment to ask 'Would you want to fix this today or prefer an estimate?'—locking in her decision timeline before he calls back with findings means she's ready to move, not deciding cold.

WIN — You showed the compressor pulling 88 amps against its 79-amp rating, and when the customer asked about replacement costs, your candid explanation—'It's already kind of that scenario where it can be a black hole as far as putting in parts'—moved them from repair-only to immediately booking the equipment specialist consultation that will land the system replacement.

WIN — You said 'It's nice having that peace of mind, knowing you don't have to do it—someone else is professionally taking care of it, and it's only $16 a month'; the customer, just shaken by watching his system completely freeze, overcame the price objection by comparing it to his Netflix subscription and signed up for the annual membership, flipping one service call into recurring revenue.

FIX — When the customer expressed concern about whether costs would be manageable, you said 'I've got a ton of testing to do'—instead, that moment called for 'Let me come back with your options: could be $200 for the capacitor, or higher if it's the compressor'—reconnecting with Decision options would've moved this forward to an estimate.

FIX — When the customer declined the membership, Ben said "Okay" instead of reconnecting to the filter maintenance issue he'd just emphasized — asking "Would quarterly filter changes be worth protecting the system?" would have offered a second option and likely flipped this into a recurring service TGL.

FIX — When Dolores mentioned that musty smell, you identified the filter-cabinet solution and explained it well, but you didn't ask if she wanted to try it—'Want to see if upgrading the filter clears that up for you?' That reconnect + commitment question right there is what would have flipped the call.

WIN — When the customer worried about putting repair money into an aging system that might fail again, you offered to bring a specialist tomorrow for repair vs. replacement pricing comparison ('put apples to apples'), turning her indecision into a committed specialist estimate visit.

FIX — Your technical explanation of R22 and Xeon parts immediately rebuilt trust after his bad prior experience, but to move from trust to a close you need to explicitly reconnect the customer's stated future replacement need ('what the new unit would cost down the road') to a concrete proposal when you present findings.

WIN — When Richard asked about senior discounts after hearing the $4,500 compressor quote, you didn't just say yes—you reconnected him to whole-system care: 'Last thing I want is for you guys to dump a ton of money into the outdoor unit and then have to call me out.' That reframe from price to integrity earned the flip from tune-up to major repair TGL.

WIN — When you said 'The actuator for that zone is burnt up—that's the only one that hasn't been replaced,' Bob had the specificity he needed to understand exactly what failed; your clinical diagnosis moved him from 'let's figure this out' to 'let's order the part and get you back here'—that precision won the TGL.

FIX — Pat mentioned her rental property down the street with five residents running out of hot water; when she expressed interest in a tankless unit, ask "Want me to have our plumbing team reach out with an estimate?" to turn her open door into a scheduled visit.

WIN — You diagnosed the bad contactor and immediately said 'your system is under warranty,' which turned the customer's urgency into action—they were already motivated, but your warranty mention made them feel confident to move forward with the $264 charge.

FIX — When the customer indicated everything's working fine, that was the moment to ask about their maintenance schedule or what matters most to them this summer—instead you went straight to the service work, missing the reconnect that surfaces upgrade options.

FIX — When Joe confirmed the outdoor unit was running and could have closed with a sale, he should have reconnected the S30 thermostat issue he'd already identified: 'Good news—your outdoor unit is working perfectly. I did notice your S30 thermostat can cause cooling problems over time—this would be a great time to upgrade to the S40 since I'm already here,' instead of pivoting straight to cancellation—that's reconnecting to build an option.

WIN — 'If I happen to notice anything of a concern, do you want me to bring it up to your attention?' got the green light to dig deeper; you turned routine maintenance into a diagnostic report with options by asking permission first — next time, come back with those options and ask for the sale on the phone instead of leaving the customer hanging.

WIN — Andrew's systematic explanation of the five-box system, the heat exchanger hot spots, and the ductwork damage from a 2006 install built trust through expertise; when he said 'I'm gonna go right up the report and get you some numbers,' the customer's immediate 'Yes, sir' showed the education had flipped the call into a TGL.

FIX — The customer asked whether cleaning was included in your service—a clear buying signal—but you only gave the price: "$149 per unit." Instead, say "I'll evaluate your system and show you if cleaning would improve efficiency." That "Building Options" step makes the upsell natural when you present findings.

WIN — Francisco's "my biggest concern is stacking money on repairs" reframed cost shock into practical risk-of-failure thinking, which opened the equipment specialist estimate visit that will generate the TGL.

WIN — "Or obviously you can just get a permanent solution, which would be replacing the system. So that way you don't have to throw good money to a bad system." When Ben voiced concern about spending $1,500 on repairs for an aging system that might need full replacement anyway—the classic cost-bleed fear—Jordan took his own objection and made it the reason TO replace, not hesitate. Reframing the objection as the solution earned the flip.

WIN — Andrew set clear expectations early, asked great discovery questions to uncover the customer's proactive mindset, and offered a concrete thermostat solution that showed technical expertise while addressing the customer's pain point with Nevada Energy.

FIX — When customer volunteers a return date (late September), lock in the callback: 'Let me schedule you a firm quote for Sept 27 so you're ready to decide.' Convert the timeline into a commitment instead of leaving it open-ended.

FIX — Get explicit commitment to the repair before checking parts: 'Arthur, if I can handle this today or tomorrow, do you want to move forward?' locks the work instead of leaving it to 'I'll call Ryan to schedule.'
FIX — When a customer leads with a failed service and refund request, acknowledge it and handle it before inspection—skipping that leaves trust broken.
FIX — Strong expectations and discovery—now transition from inspection findings to options you can close on instead of just reporting.

WIN — When the customer hesitated on replacement cost, Benjamin pivoted to 'I can just get a guy out here real quick in 30 to 45 minutes today' — offering same-day specialist availability instead of debating price removed the timeline barrier and flipped this into a TGL.

FIX — When the customer revealed one unit was completely down with 91-degree temps, Cole said 'Gotcha' but didn't ask 'What troubleshooting have you guys already tried?'—that answer would've given him specifics to anchor his post-inspection recommendations to instead of generic findings.

FIX — When the supervisor said it might be cheaper to replace the entire system, Joe replied 'Maybe. Let me write everything out.' He missed the moment to say 'Great question—let me show you the cost to replace just the compressor with refrigerant versus a whole new system so you can decide' and actually present those options then (Building Options).

WIN — Noah said 'You can find somebody else, but you don't really know who you're getting' when customer hesitated on replacement cost, then offered a free specialist estimate—immediately flipped the objection into an estimate TGL.

FIX — When the customer mentioned she hadn't cleaned her AC in six years, that's your opening for a cleaning proposal; reconnect that discovery with 'That's definitely buildup we should address today' and you'll have your positioning for a service recommendation.

FIX — When Felipe asked "¿Cuánto es?" about the maintenance plan, you explained the features but didn't ask a closing question like "Would four scheduled visits give you peace of mind on this 2-year-old system?"; instead you moved straight to the inspection—that reconnecting question is what would've flipped this to a yes.
FIX — Customer said they couldn't move in without AC but you didn't Reconnect to that urgency at close - should've said 'I'll call your landlord with options by end of day so you can move in.'

FIX — When the customer expressed worry about replacement being expensive, Noah said 'I'll give you all your options, so don't even worry,' but reconnecting with a question like 'What matters most to you—finding an affordable solution or keeping the system running long-term?' would have helped him come back with options tailored to their actual budget.
WIN — Juan earned the proposal by taking the customer's ductwork concern seriously and committing to a comprehensive, well-documented evaluation.
FIX — Ask if they're worried the capacitor will fail again; when customers mention past AC failures, their fear is where the close lives.
FIX — During the inspection, walk the customer through findings as you discover them rather than waiting for the report, so they're primed and ready when you present options for approval.
